stage.name: Security & Wallets
stage.description: BIP39/44 seed derivation, wallet types, attack vectors, multisig setups, and XRPL account security.

elements.content: BIP39 — Seed Phrase Standard
How 12 words become the key to your entire wallet
1

Entropy Source

128–256 bits of randomness from a secure source
2

Word Mapping

2,048-word BIP39 wordlist maps bits to words
3

12–24 Words

Your seed phrase — the master key to all accounts
4

PBKDF2 Hash

Seed phrase + optional passphrase → 512-bit master seed
5

HD Tree

BIP32 derives unlimited child keys from master seed

actions.text: Your seed phrase is not just a password — it's the cryptographic master key to everything in your wallet, forever.
BIP39 is the standard that turns 12 to 24 English words into a deterministic wallet.
Step one: 128 to 256 bits of true randomness from a cryptographically secure source. Your hardware wallet generates this internally.
Step two: the BIP39 wordlist maps those bits to human-readable words from a fixed 2048-word list. Easy to write down, harder to mistype than hex.
Your 12 to 24 words ARE the master key. Anyone who has them has everything. Write them down once, store them securely offline.
PBKDF2 hashing: your seed phrase combined with an optional passphrase gets hashed into a 512-bit master seed. The passphrase adds a 25th word layer of security.
BIP32 HD derivation: from that master seed, unlimited child keys are derived deterministically. One seed, unlimited accounts.

elements.content: BIP44 Derivation Paths
The address structure that organizes your entire wallet
m / 44' / 144' / 0' / 0 / 0

m = master seed · 44' = BIP44 purpose · 144' = XRPL coin type
0' = account 0 · 0 = external chain · 0 = address index

Bitcoin uses coin type 0 · Ethereum uses 60 · XRPL uses 144
Hardened derivation (marked with ') means child keys cannot be derived from the parent public key — extra security layer.

actions.text: BIP44 standardizes how wallets derive addresses — so the same seed phrase works across different wallets and blockchains.
The derivation path tells your wallet exactly which account and address to generate.
For XRPL: m slash 44 prime slash 144 prime slash 0 prime slash 0 slash 0. Each segment specifies purpose, coin type, account, chain, and address index.

The prime marks indicate hardened derivation — child keys cannot be derived from the parent public key alone, adding security. 144 is XRPL's registered coin type. This is why your Ledger knows where your XRPL address is without you telling it.
Hardened derivation is a critical security feature. Without it, if an attacker learns one derived private key, they could derive all sibling keys.

elements.content: Hot vs Cold Storage
Not your keys, not your coins — and how to hold your keys

🔥 Hot Wallets

• Connected to internet

• Private key in software/browser

• Convenient for daily use

• MetaMask, XUMM, Coinbase Wallet

• Vulnerable to malware, phishing

• Only keep small amounts
VS

🔒 Cold Wallets

• Never connected to internet

• Private key on dedicated hardware

• Sign transactions offline

• Ledger, Trezor, Coldcard

• Physical theft = only vector

• Store majority of holdings

actions.text: Not your keys, not your coins. Let's talk about what that actually means in practice.
Hot wallets are convenient but exposed. Cold wallets are secure but less convenient. Know when to use each.
Hot wallets: connected to the internet, private key in software or browser extension. Convenient for daily use. MetaMask, Xumm — these are hot wallets. The risk: internet exposure, phishing, malware.

Cold wallets: never connected to the internet. Private key stored on a dedicated hardware device. You sign transactions offline and broadcast only the signed transaction. Ledger and Trezor. For serious holdings, cold storage is non-negotiable.

elements.content: Attack Vectors & Defenses
The threats are real — here is how to counter them

The $5 Wrench Attack

No amount of cryptography protects you from physical coercion.

Solutions:
• Passphrase (25th word): Create a hidden wallet. Give up the decoy wallet with small funds.
• Multisig: No single person can authorize. Co-signers in different locations.
• Never disclose holdings publicly.

Common Attack Vectors

• Phishing: Fake Ledger/MetaMask emails asking for seed phrase (NEVER share)
• Clipboard hijacking: Malware swaps your copy-pasted address
• SIM swap: Attacker controls your phone number, defeats SMS 2FA
• Fake wallets: Downloaded from unofficial sources

Hardware key always beats SMS 2FA.

actions.text: Understanding attack vectors is how you build real security — not just theoretical security.
The threats range from sophisticated technical attacks to basic social engineering — and social engineering works on technically smart people too.

The five dollar wrench attack: if someone knows you hold significant crypto and is willing to threaten physical harm, no cryptography protects you. Solutions: passphrase on your seed, decoy wallets with small amounts, operational security about your holdings.

Common attack vectors: phishing — fake Ledger or MetaMask emails asking for your seed phrase. Clipboard hijacking — malware that replaces copied wallet addresses. Supply chain attacks — modified hardware wallets or compromised software packages.

elements.content: Multisig Security
For serious holdings: no single point of failure

2-of-3
Most common
1

M-of-N Setup

Require M signatures from N total keys. 2-of-3 means any 2 of 3 signers can authorize.
2

Geographic Split

Store keys in different cities/countries. No single point of failure.
3

Hardware Keys

Each signer uses a hardware wallet. No single device controls funds.
4

Timelock

Optional: add a time delay to large transactions for review window.

actions.text: Multisig is the gold standard for securing significant holdings. No single point of failure.
M-of-N means M signatures from N possible keys are required to authorize a transaction.
M-of-N setup: 2-of-3 is the most common. Any 2 of your 3 signing keys can authorize a transaction. One key can be lost without losing access.
Geographic split: store your signing keys in different physical locations — different cities, different countries. A flood or fire can't wipe out all three.
Hardware keys: each signer uses a dedicated hardware wallet. Even if an attacker compromises one device, they can't move funds without a second signature.
Timelock: optionally add a time delay to large transactions. This creates a review window — you have time to cancel a fraudulent transaction before it executes.

elements.content: XRPL Account Security
Platform-specific features every OnlyCrypto member should know

XRPL Account Flags

RequireDestTag: prevents lost funds from missing tags. DisableMasterKey: force multisig, deactivate master key. Blackhole: set signer weight to 0 — irrecoverable.

Destination Tags

Required for exchange deposits. Tag identifies which user on a shared wallet address. Missing tag = lost funds — many exchanges cannot recover.

Account Reserve

1 XRP minimum reserve to activate account. Each trust line and offer costs additional reserve. Reserve is returned if objects are deleted.

actions.text: XRPL has built-in account security features that every OnlyCrypto member should know about.
These are protocol-level protections, not third-party tools — they're built directly into how the ledger works.

XRPL account flags: RequireDestTag prevents funds from being lost to missing destination tags. DisableMasterKey forces multisig, removing the single-key vulnerability. These are enforced by the ledger itself.

Destination tags: required for exchange deposits. The tag identifies which user is sending funds to a shared wallet address. Missing your destination tag on an exchange deposit can mean lost funds — always check.

Account reserve: 1 XRP minimum to activate an XRPL account. Each trust line and offer you create requires an additional reserve. This prevents spam accounts and ensures every address on the ledger has skin in the game.

elements.content: Seed Phrase Best Practices
The checklist every crypto holder must follow
1

Generate Offline

Use a hardware wallet or air-gapped device to generate keys. Never on an internet-connected machine.
2

Write on Metal

Paper burns and floods. Cryptosteel or similar metal backups survive house fires.
3

Test Restore

Before storing any funds, test restoring from your seed phrase. Verify it works.
4

Secure Location

Separate your seed from your hardware wallet. Bank vault, trusted family member, or geographic split.

actions.text: Seed phrase best practices are non-negotiable. Most crypto losses come from human error, not sophisticated hacks.
Follow this checklist and you will avoid 99% of the ways people lose their holdings.
Generate offline: always generate seed phrases on a hardware wallet or air-gapped device. Never on a phone, browser, or website. If it touched the internet during generation, consider it compromised.
Write on metal, not paper. Paper burns. Paper floods. Metal backup solutions like Cryptosteel survive house fires and water damage. Your seed phrase should outlast you if needed.

Test restore before trusting. Before storing any funds, restore your wallet from the seed phrase on a second device. Verify the addresses match. Many people discover errors only when they actually need recovery — too late.

Secure location, separate from device. Your hardware wallet and your seed phrase backup should never be in the same place. Bank vault, trusted family member in another city, or a professional custody service.

content.html:
Wallet Security Strength Checker

Score your wallet setup against best practices. Spot weaknesses before attackers do.

actions.text: Let's explore this interactively. Use the simulation below to experiment with the concepts we just covered.
Try different inputs and see how the outputs change. Hands-on exploration builds intuition faster than reading.

questions.question: What does BIP39 define?

options.label: The format of Bitcoin block headers
The mnemonic seed phrase standard for HD wallets

questions.analysis: BIP39 defines 12–24 word mnemonic seed phrases. The words are hashed via PBKDF2 to generate a 512-bit master seed.

questions.question: What is the BIP44 derivation path for the first XRPL account?
questions.analysis: XRPL's coin type is 144 (registered in SLIP-0044). The full path is m/44'/144'/0'/0/0 for the first account.

questions.question: A hardware wallet protects your funds even if the device is stolen because:

options.label: The seed phrase is encrypted with your PIN
Private keys never leave the device unencrypted
The device remotely wipes after 3 failed PIN attempts

questions.analysis: Hardware wallets sign transactions internally — private keys are generated and stored on-chip and never transmitted. The attacker would need both the device AND your PIN.

questions.question: Which TWO measures directly protect against the '$5 wrench attack' (physical coercion)?

options.label: Using a passphrase (25th word) on a separate decoy wallet
Storing your seed phrase in a fireproof safe
Using a multisig setup with geographically distributed signers
Using a hardware wallet instead of a hot wallet

questions.analysis: A passphrase creates a hidden wallet — give up the decoy seed. Multisig with distant co-signers means no single person can authorize. Fireproof safes and hardware wallets protect against digital attacks, not physical coercion.

questions.question: You receive an email from 'Ledger Support' saying your device has been compromised and you must enter your 24-word seed phrase on their website. Identify 3 red flags and explain what you should do.
questions.commentPrompt: Full marks for: (1) Ledger/any company will NEVER ask for your seed phrase, (2) the seed phrase should never be typed into any website or device other than the hardware wallet itself, (3) this is a phishing attack — delete/report it. Bonus: check the actual domain, contact support through official channels.

actions.text: Time to test your knowledge! Answer each question carefully — you can review the material after the quiz.
Take your time with these questions. The goal is to solidify your understanding, not just pass the quiz.
